chunk_summary: Infants with congenital heart disease (CHD) require high-calorie human milk feeding strategies to address energy needs and limited feeding stamina, including time-limited breastfeeding, milk fortification, and supplemental NG feeds.
learning_objectives: Understand the challenges of breastfeeding infants with congenital heart disease (CHD), including energy needs and feeding stamina.
Learn strategies for fortifying expressed human milk to increase caloric density while managing fluid restrictions.
Recognize the importance of maternal milk supply protection and coordination with cardiology for safe feeding thresholds.
key_facts: Infants with CHD often tire quickly at the breast, leading to prolonged feeds with low milk transfer and poor weight gain.
Time-limited breastfeeding (10–15 minutes per side) followed by expressed milk top-ups is recommended to meet caloric needs.
Fortification of expressed human milk may be necessary to increase caloric density, especially for infants on fluid restriction or diuretic therapy.
Maternal milk supply should be protected with 8–10 milk removals per day, including regular expression.
Supplemental NG feeds can deliver additional nutrition while preserving bonding opportunities at the breast.
Caregivers should monitor for warning signs such as worsening tachypnea, cyanosis, poor perfusion, or reduced urine output.
reference_quote: Infants with congenital heart disease often have a mismatch between high energy needs and limited feeding stamina, requiring specialized feeding plans to conserve energy and ensure adequate calories. (Source: CDC)
document_key_topics: Breastfeeding adaptations for infants with congenital heart disease
Maternal milk supply protection in CHD cases
